name: Bhartrhari
dates_notes: Dated by the Chinese pilgrim I-tsing's reference; the Vākyapadīya survives.
core_thesis: Bhartṛhari is a grammarian-philosopher in the Pāṇinian tradition, and his thesis is named with care: *śabda-advaita*, word-non-dualism. The ultimate is *śabda-brahman* — an undivided word-principle from which the manifest world of meanings, objects, and cognitions arises by *vivarta* (apparent transformation, not real modification). The opening verse of the *Vākyapadīya* fixes this ([*Vākyapadīya* 1.1](cite://bhartrhari/vakyapadiya/1.1), *Brahma-Kāṇḍa*): *anādi-nidhanaṃ brahma śabda-tattvaṃ yad akṣaram / vivartate 'rtha-bhāvena prakriyā jagato yataḥ* — "That imperishable word-reality, *brahman*, beginningless and endless, appears in the form of objects; from it the world-process [proceeds]." The verb *vivartate* is load-bearing: appearance, not transformation. A second thesis follows. There is no cognition (*pratyaya*) unsuffused by *śabda*; awareness is intrinsically linguistic ([*Vākyapadīya* 1.131–134](cite://bhartrhari/vakyapadiya/1.131-134)). A third thesis concerns the units of meaning. The integral *vākya* (sentence) is meaning-bearing and yields a single instantaneous *pratibhā* (intuitive flash) ([*Vākyapadīya* 2.143–152](cite://bhartrhari/vakyapadiya/2.143-152), *Vākya-Kāṇḍa*); the analyzed *pada* and *varṇa* are abstractions extracted by grammatical analysis, not the basic semantic atoms. A fourth thesis orders the descent of speech: *paśyantī* (seeing), *madhyamā* (intermediate, mental), *vaikharī* (articulated) ([*Vākyapadīya* 1.142–144](cite://bhartrhari/vakyapadiya/1.142-144) and *vṛtti* thereon). The work is grammatical in genre, but its *vivarta* idiom and its monistic word-ontology pass directly into Maṇḍana (*Brahma-Siddhi*, *brahma-kāṇḍa*) and from Maṇḍana into the technical Advaita lexicon.

engaged_works.title: Vakyapadiya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; the foundational treatise of Bhartṛhari's grammatical-philosophical system.

ascription_evidence.description: Universal modern consensus on Bhartṛhari's authorship of the Vākyapadīya.

engaged_works.summary: A *prakaraṇa* in three *kāṇḍas* — hence the alternative title *Trikāṇḍī*. Each *kāṇḍa* takes one stratum of grammatical philosophy. *Brahma-Kāṇḍa* ([*Vākyapadīya* 1](cite://bhartrhari/vakyapadiya/1), also *Āgama-Samuccaya*) develops the metaphysics of *śabda-brahman* (1.1–22), the *sphoṭa* doctrine of meaning-bearing linguistic units (1.93–125), and the three levels of *vāk* (1.142–144). *Vākya-Kāṇḍa* ([*Vākyapadīya* 2](cite://bhartrhari/vakyapadiya/2)) defends the primacy of the integral sentence over its analytic parts (2.1–42), surveys competing theories of meaning (2.42–142), and gives the doctrine of *pratibhā* (the unified meaning-flash, at 2.143–152). *Pada-Kāṇḍa* ([*Vākyapadīya* 3](cite://bhartrhari/vakyapadiya/3), also *Prakīrṇaka*) analyzes nominal categories one at a time across its fourteen *samuddeśa*-sections: *jāti* (3.1), *dravya* (3.2), *saṃbandha* (3.3), *sādhana* (3.7), *kriyā* (3.8), *kāla* (3.9), *puruṣa* (3.10), *saṃkhyā* (3.11). Bhartṛhari's own *vṛtti* survives on *kāṇḍas* 1 and 2; its authorship on *kāṇḍa* 1 is contested (Aklujkar treats it as Bhartṛhari's own; others remain skeptical). Two doctrinal moves of the *Vākyapadīya* propagate widely beyond grammatical philosophy. The *vivarta* terminology — *brahman* appearing as the manifold without itself undergoing real change ([*Vākyapadīya* 1.1](cite://bhartrhari/vakyapadiya/1.1)) — supplies the causal idiom that Maṇḍana and the later Advaitins prefer against *pariṇāma*. The conception of an undifferentiated linguistic absolute grounding all cognitive content ([*Vākyapadīya* 1.131–134](cite://bhartrhari/vakyapadiya/1.131-134)) supplies a model for an absolute that is the ground of *pramāṇa*-activity itself.
